text: {"matches":[{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":14,"rationale":"Both state they are the first thematic analysis of circulating COVID‑19 misinformation in Nigeria."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":12,"rationale":"Both identify the leading false claim that COVID‑19 is not real and politicians misuse funds."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":42,"rationale":"Both describe the most frequently reported statement that COVID‑19 is not real."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":12,"toIndex":42,"rationale":"Both report the leading claim that COVID‑19 was not real and politicians took advantage of the situation."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":55,"rationale":"Both assert the leading conspiracy theory that SARS‑CoV‑2 does not exist and the pandemic response is used for financial gain."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":12,"toIndex":55,"rationale":"Both describe the leading conspiracy theory that the virus does not exist and is a vehicle for misusing funds."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":10,"toIndex":27,"rationale":"Both give identical details: 22 focus groups, 24 key informant interviews, 178 participants, Feb 1‑8 2021."},{"type":"broader","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":54,"rationale":"Claim [1] includes universal exposure to misinformation about COVID‑19 *and* the vaccine, whereas [54] mentions only COVID‑19 misinformation."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":11,"toIndex":36,"rationale":"Claim [11] says all participants heard some misinformation; [36] expands to include conspiracy theories or misinformation."},{"type":"broader","fromIndex":23,"toIndex":24,"rationale":"Conspiracy theories are a specific type of misinformation; therefore [24] is a narrower subset of [23]."},{"type":"broader","fromIndex":18,"toIndex":19,"rationale":"[18] lists many determinants of vaccine coverage; [19] focuses solely on vaccine acceptance as one determinant."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":62,"toIndex":63,"rationale":"Both claim that highlighting low side‑effect risk and high efficacy promotes vaccine acceptance."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":64,"toIndex":62,"rationale":"[64] provides a specific hypothetical scenario (5 % side‑effects, 75 % efficacy) whereas [62] makes a general statement about such messaging."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":46,"toIndex":59,"rationale":"Both report that information from WHO, NCDC, and NPHCDA is perceived as trustworthy/reliable by participants."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":60,"toIndex":61,"rationale":"[60] links traditional media use to lower belief in conspiracies; [61] links digital media exposure to higher belief."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":9,"toIndex":56,"rationale":"Both state that conspiracy/hoax theories drive vaccine hesitancy/refusal, but [56] specifies hoax‑related theories."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":5,"toIndex":9,"rationale":"[5] describes vaccine hesitancy as a global threat; [9] emphasizes that misinformation drives hesitancy."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":4,"rationale":"[3] suggests tracking public opinion will help counter hesitancy; [4] proposes adaptive messages to counter misinformation."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":7,"toIndex":21,"rationale":"Both note that improved global vaccine supply in H2 2021 will shift coverage drivers to demand/acceptance, though phrasing differs."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":45,"toIndex":47,"rationale":"[45] lists radio, TV, and social media as main information sources; [47] focuses on trust in radio and TV."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":45,"toIndex":48,"rationale":"[45] lists media sources; [48] highlights participants’ view that social media information is unreliable."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":57,"toIndex":58,"rationale":"[57] reports higher skepticism among Igbos in Southeast Nigeria; [58] reports lower vaccine intention in the same region."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":69,"toIndex":70,"rationale":"[69] links government distrust to hesitancy; [70] calls for adaptive strategies to address that hesitancy."}]}
